q: Ionic bonding results from the transfer of electrons from an atom having small ionisation energy to an atom having a large electron affinity.
Mostly ionic bonding takes place when the difference in electronegativity between two atoms is less than $1.8$.
In ionic bonding, a metal loses electrons to form positively charged ions called cations.
The number of lost or gained electrons by an atom during ionic bonding is the same as the valency of that element.
Ionic compounds are always neutral because electrons loss always equals electrons gain in an electron transfer process.
In ionic compounds, the cations and anions are held together by strong electrostatic attraction.
The melting points of ionic compounds are higher when the charge on the ions is greater.
In solid ionic compounds, ions can move freely and hence they conduct electricity.
The crystalline structure of an ionic solid is called a crystal lattice.
The cubic unit cell has three types: simple cubic ($sc$), face-centred cubic ($fcc$), and body-centred cubic ($bcc$).
Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) contains only one $Na^{+}$ ion and one $Cl^{-}$ ion in its structure.
The term coordination number is used to express the number of ions surrounding the central ion in the lattice.
Unit cell is the smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice that shows the three-dimensional pattern of the entire crystal.
A face-centred cubic unit cell is a cubic cell with an atom on each corner and an atom at the centre of the cube.
In the case of magnesium oxide ($MgO$), the electronegativity difference is $2.3$.
Electrostatic attraction is the attraction between a negatively charged ion and a positively charged ion.
Sodium fluoride can be used in water fluoridation.
Potassium iodide is added to 'iodised' salt for thyroid health.
Metal salts such as strontium carbonate and calcium chloride are used in fireworks.
Sodium, calcium, potassium, chloride, phosphate, and magnesium ions are all electrolytes.
